course_info.title: Design Thinking and Process
prerequisites: UX101 - Introduction to User Experience Design

class_schedule.topic: Origins and Philosophy of Design Thinking
learning_objectives: Trace the origins of design thinking from IDEO and Stanford d.school
Articulate the five phases of the design thinking process
Distinguish design thinking from traditional problem-solving approaches
readings: Change by Design by Tim Brown, Ch. 1-3
Stanford d.school: An Introduction to Design Thinking Process Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Design thinking is a human-centered approach to innovation that draws from the designer's toolkit to integrate the needs of people, the possibilities of technology, and the requirements for business success.
key_concepts: The five phases: Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test
History: from industrial design to business strategy
Divergent vs. convergent thinking in each phase
Wicked problems and why linear approaches fail
The mindset: human-centered, collaborative, optimistic, experimental
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mini design sprint: class tackles a real campus wayfinding problem using all five phases in 30 minutes

review.value: Empathize (understand users), Define (frame the problem), Ideate (generate solutions), Prototype (make it tangible), Test (learn from users)
Problems with no single correct solution, where the problem definition itself changes as you explore solutions — exactly where design thinking excels

class_schedule.topic: Empathy: The Foundation of Design Thinking
learning_objectives: Conduct empathy interviews using structured techniques
Build empathy maps from interview data
Distinguish empathy from sympathy in the design context
readings: Creative Confidence by Tom Kelley & David Kelley, Ch. 1-2
IDEO Field Guide to Human-Centered Design, Empathy section

lecture_notes.introduction: Empathy is the cornerstone of design thinking. Before we can solve a problem, we must deeply understand the people experiencing it — their needs, emotions, motivations, and context.
key_concepts: Empathy interviews: listening to stories, not opinions
Observation techniques: body language, environment, workarounds
Empathy maps: Says, Thinks, Does, Feels quadrants
Extreme users and lead users as innovation sources
The difference between stated needs and latent needs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Paired empathy interview exercise: students interview each other about their morning routine, then build empathy maps on the whiteboard

review.value: Four-quadrant tool (Says, Thinks, Does, Feels) that captures user insights from interviews and observations to build shared understanding
Needs users cannot articulate directly — discovered through observing behavior and workarounds rather than asking what they want

learning_objectives: Synthesize research data into clear problem statements
Write effective Point-of-View (POV) statements and How Might We questions
Use affinity mapping to identify patterns in qualitative data
readings: Gamestorming by Dave Gray, Ch. on Affinity Mapping

lecture_notes.introduction: The Define phase transforms raw empathy data into a clear, actionable problem statement. A well-framed problem is half-solved.
key_concepts: Affinity diagramming: clustering observations into themes
Point-of-View statement format: [User] needs [need] because [insight]
How Might We (HMW) questions: reframing problems as opportunities
Avoiding solution-framing in problem statements
Scope management: narrowing to an actionable problem space
lecture_notes.demonstration: Class synthesis exercise using data from previous empathy interviews — building affinity diagrams on the wall and crafting POV statements
setup_requirements: Post-it notes from previous empathy exercise
Large wall or board space for affinity mapping

review.value: [User] needs [need] because [surprising insight] — a actionable problem statement that captures who, what, and why
Questions that reframe problems as opportunities for design — broad enough to allow creative solutions, specific enough to guide ideation

learning_objectives: Facilitate structured brainstorming sessions using multiple ideation techniques
Apply lateral thinking methods to generate non-obvious solutions
Evaluate and prioritize ideas using structured selection methods
readings: Creative Confidence by Tom Kelley & David Kelley, Ch. 3-4
Thinkertoys by Michael Michalko, selected methods

lecture_notes.introduction: Ideation is where design thinking generates its creative power. The goal is quantity and wild variety before filtering for quality.
key_concepts: Rules of brainstorming: defer judgment, build on ideas, go for quantity
Brainwriting (6-3-5 method) for introverted ideation
SCAMPER technique: Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse
Crazy Eights: rapid sketching to externalize ideas
Dot voting and impact/effort matrix for idea selection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full ideation workshop using a real design brief — brainwriting, crazy eights, and dot voting in sequence
setup_requirements: A4 paper for Crazy Eights templates
Thick markers (no detail, forces big-picture thinking)

review.value: Rapid sketching exercise where participants draw 8 distinct ideas in 8 minutes — forces quick thinking and prevents over-investing in one concept
2x2 grid plotting ideas by potential impact (high/low) and implementation effort (high/low) — prioritize high-impact, low-effort ideas first

learning_objectives: Build low-fidelity prototypes rapidly using appropriate materials
Match prototype fidelity to the question being tested
Create storyboards that communicate user scenarios effectively
readings: Sprint by Jake Knapp, Ch. on Prototyping
Prototyping: A Practitioner's Guide by Todd Zaki Warfel, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: A prototype is anything a user can interact with to test an idea. The goal is to learn quickly and cheaply — not to build a polished product.
key_concepts: Fidelity spectrum: paper sketches to clickable mockups to coded prototypes
Choosing the right fidelity for the question you are testing
Paper prototyping techniques for rapid iteration
Storyboarding: visualizing the user journey with a proposed solution
Wizard of Oz prototyping for testing complex interactions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hands-on paper prototyping session — students build and test paper prototypes of a mobile app in 20 minutes

review.value: Low fidelity (paper, sketches) tests concepts and flows; medium fidelity (wireframes) tests layout and navigation; high fidelity (polished mockups) tests visual design and microinteractions
A human secretly operates the system behind the scenes while the user thinks they are interacting with technology — tests concepts before building anything

learning_objectives: Plan and conduct a usability test session with a prototype
Write effective task scenarios for prototype testing
Synthesize test findings into design iterations
readings: Rocket Surgery Made Easy by Steve Krug, Ch. 1-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing closes the design thinking loop. Every prototype test generates insights that feed back into empathy, redefine the problem, or refine the solution.
key_concepts: Usability testing basics: facilitator, observer, note-taker roles
Writing task scenarios: realistic, goal-oriented, non-leading
Think-aloud protocol and its variations
Capturing and prioritizing findings across multiple test sessions
Iterating based on test results: pivot, persevere, or zoom in
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live usability test of student paper prototypes from last session — class observes and takes notes, then debriefs together

review.value: Testing technique where users verbalize their thoughts, expectations, and reactions while interacting with a prototype — reveals mental models and confusion in real time
A realistic narrative giving the user a goal to accomplish with the prototype without telling them how — 'You want to find a vegetarian restaurant near campus for dinner tonight'

questions.question: Explain the difference between a stated need and a latent need. Provide an example of each from a hypothetical user research study about grocery shopping.
questions.answer: A stated need is what users explicitly say they want (e.g., 'I want a faster checkout process'). A latent need is something users cannot articulate but that emerges from observing their behavior (e.g., observing that users frequently reorganize items in their cart by meal, revealing a latent need for meal-based shopping organization). Stated needs come from asking; latent needs come from observing.

rubric.key: Correct definition of stated need with example
Correct definition of latent need with example
Explains how each is discovered (asking vs. observing)

questions.question: Write a Point-of-View (POV) statement and one How Might We (HMW) question for the following scenario: College students struggle to find healthy, affordable meals near campus during late-night study sessions.
questions.answer: POV: College students who study late at night need access to healthy, affordable food options near campus because the only options currently available are fast food and vending machines, which undermine their energy and academic performance. HMW: How might we make nutritious food accessible and affordable for students during late-night study hours?

rubric.key: HMW is open-ended and not solution-prescriptive
Both are grounded in the provided scenario

questions.question: Describe the SCAMPER ideation technique. Choose three of the seven prompts and show how you would apply each to redesign a traditional library study room.
questions.answer: SCAMPER is a structured ideation technique using seven prompts: Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse. Examples for a library study room: Substitute — replace fixed desks with modular standing/sitting furniture. Combine — merge the study room with a cafe for food access during study. Eliminate — remove the need for physical booking by using occupancy sensors and a real-time availability app.

rubric.key: All seven SCAMPER prompts listed correctly
Three prompts applied with specific, creative ideas
Ideas are relevant to the study room context

questions.question: A product team is deciding whether to build a paper prototype or a high-fidelity clickable prototype for their first round of user testing. What factors should guide this decision? When is each appropriate?
questions.answer: The decision depends on what question you are trying to answer. Paper prototypes are appropriate early in the process when testing concepts, information architecture, and basic flows — they are fast to create, encourage honest feedback (users feel comfortable criticizing rough work), and prevent premature commitment to visual design. High-fidelity prototypes are appropriate when testing visual design, microinteractions, or brand perception, or when stakeholders need convincing. Key factors: stage of the project, type of question being tested, time and resources available, and audience for the test.

rubric.key: Identifies 'what question are you testing' as the key factor
Correctly describes when paper prototypes are appropriate
Correctly describes when high-fidelity prototypes are appropriate
Mentions additional factors like time, resources, audience

major_assignments.title: Design Thinking Sprint Project
major_assignments.description: Apply the complete design thinking process to a real problem affecting students on campus. Work through all five phases — Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test — and document each phase with evidence of your process and findings.
objectives: Demonstrate mastery of all five design thinking phases
Conduct real empathy research with at least 5 participants
Generate and evaluate at least 30 distinct ideas
Build and test a prototype with at least 3 users
deliverables: Process book (15-20 pages) documenting all five phases with evidence
Empathy maps and research synthesis artifacts
POV statement and at least 10 HMW questions
Photos/scans of all ideation artifacts (brainstorm outputs, crazy eights)
Prototype (physical or digital) and usability test findings report

criteria: At least 5 empathy interviews conducted with real users
Empathy maps demonstrate genuine understanding
Observations go beyond stated needs to latent needs
Research is well-documented with quotes and observations
Clear synthesis from research data to problem statement
POV statement follows correct format and is insightful
HMW questions are well-scoped and generative
Problem framing avoids premature solutions
At least 30 distinct ideas generated using multiple techniques
Evidence of both divergent and convergent thinking
Selection criteria are explicit and justified
Final concept builds on ideation insights
Prototype fidelity matches the question being tested
At least 3 users tested with realistic task scenarios
Findings are synthesized with clear patterns identified
Iteration based on findings is documented
Process book is well-organized and visually clear
Each phase transition is explained and justified
Reflections on what was learned at each phase
Professional presentation quality
Authentic application of all five design thinking phases with real users
Strong research leading to insightful problem definition
Creative and varied ideation with structured evaluation
Prototype tested and iterated based on real user feedback

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on design thinking concepts, methods, and frameworks

class_participation.description: Active engagement in workshops, critiques, and collaborative exercises

design_sprint_project.description: End-to-end design thinking sprint applying all five phases to a real problem

weekly_exercises.description: Individual and team exercises practicing empathy, ideation, and prototyping techniques

final_reflection.description: Reflective essay and presentation on design thinking process learnings and personal growth

course_policies.attendance: This is a workshop-based course. Attendance is mandatory. More than 2 unexcused absences will result in a full letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work is penalized 10% per day. Process book phases submitted late disrupt the sequential learning structure and will not receive feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: All workshop activities are collaborative. The design sprint project is individual but must involve real external participants for research and testing.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Your process must be authentic. Fabricating interview data or test results is academic fraud. All empathy research must use real participants with informed consent.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with Figma, physical prototyping supplies (provided in class), and access to the course Miro workspace.
